page: Google Cloud Platform g4-standard-12 is a graphics-optimized virtual machine instance configured with 12 dedicated x86_64 vCPUs and 45.0 GB of system memory. This configuration yields a memory-to-core ratio of 3.75 GB per vCPU. Acceleration is provided by an NVIDIA RTX Pro 6000 GPU, which is allocated as a fractional resource representing one-quarter of the physical graphics processor.

This instance type does not include local NVMe SSD storage, meaning all storage requirements must be met through network-attached block storage. Additionally, the instance does not include any complimentary public IPv4 addresses, which may require separate provisioning for external network connectivity. The dedicated CPU allocation ensures consistent processing capabilities without resource sharing at the hypervisor level.

The fractional GPU allocation provides a cost-efficient entry point for workloads that require hardware acceleration but do not demand a full dedicated GPU. This resource density makes the instance appropriate for tasks such as entry-level 3D rendering, remote workstation hosting, model inference, and graphics-intensive development environments.
description: Google Cloud Platform g4-standard-12 is a graphics-optimized virtual machine featuring 12 dedicated x86_64 vCPUs and 45.0 GB of system memory, establishing a ratio of 3.75 GB of RAM per core. The instance is equipped with a fractional NVIDIA RTX Pro 6000 GPU, representing one-quarter of the physical graphics processor. This hardware profile does not include local storage, requiring network-attached storage solutions, and provides zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses. The dedicated CPU allocation ensures stable compute performance. This instance is designed for workloads requiring moderate GPU acceleration, such as 3D rendering, remote desktop hosting, and light machine learning inference, offering a cost-efficient alternative to full-GPU instances by utilizing fractional hardware resources.

tagline: An x86_64 virtual machine featuring a fractional graphics processor and dedicated compute resources for accelerated workloads without full-GPU overhead.
